name: El Pimpi
quote: Admire the sherry barrels signed by celebrities and enjoy some of the best cheese and charcuterie in town.
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: Uvedoble
quote: It's a tiny tapas bar, so either get there early or make a reservation in advance. Their ajo blanco is a delicious choice!
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: La Tranca
quote: Fun and filled with locals, La Tranca does cold beer and yummy empanadas. It's a place where a lot of younger people hang out, and a fun place to have a quick beer before something more substantial.
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: Los Gatos
quote: Visit for excellent tostas (delicious toppings on toasted bread) and vermouth on tap! Started in Madrid, Los Gatos is as Madrileño as it gets but the owners moved here and brought it with them.
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: Las Merchanas
quote: Probably one of the most peculiar bars in all of Spain, this Semana Santa themed bar goes to show just how much the locals love the Easter traditions — chow down on yummy traditional tapas and drinks amid incense and virgins adorning the space.
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: El Tapeo de Cervantes
quote: Their tapas are high quality and inventive, and they have some great wines by the glass. Desserts are great too — so save room!
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: Mesón Mariano
quote: The most famous dish here is the artichokes, which are prepared just about any way possible — grilled, fried, breaded, candied, or cooked Montillana-style with jamón ibérico. They're delicious… as is everything!
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: Bar La Comba
quote: Venture out to Bar La Comba for some of Malaga's yummiest fried fish, sold in paper cones. You can grab and go, or eat at their sidewalk tables — super casual but don't miss it if you love fried fish!
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: Marisquería Casa Vicente
quote: Casa Vicente is known for its shrimp — it's been selling them since the 1960s — served by the plateful with a pink bucket on the side for the shells. Sprinkled with salt and a touch of squeezed lemon, they are perfect with a cold beer any time of day.
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: Pez Tomillo
quote: Pez Tomillo is far from a shack but it is a great restaurant with modern interpretations of Malaga classics, and really yummy seafood too.
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: El Cabra
quote: Their espetos de sardinas and roasted red peppers are to die for. I try to make a trip here every time I come to Malaga — even if it means taking a taxi straight to the train station after lunch!
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: El Tintero
quote: The quality of food isn't quite that of the others, but the experience is completely unique. Waiters will walk around, auctioning off their plates, fresh from the grill. Most plates cost €8.50. You accept what you want and at the end they count your plates for the bill.
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: Antigua Casa de Guardia
quote: They're known for their old barrels filled with Malaga's fortified wines. Try the pajarete (sweet, but not overly so) or a seco trasañejo for something dry — with a kick!
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: Los Patios de Beatas
quote: This wine shop/bar/restaurant serves a huge variety of wines, many of them by the glass. If you stay for dinner, you can order their tasting menu and enjoy a wine pairing. Don't miss the local Malaga dry wines.
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: Casa Aranda
quote: This is one of the most classic places in town to have churros. So many people love them that they have an entire little alleyway serving them all day long.
article_title: Where to Eat in Malaga in 2024: The Ultimate Food Guide

name: Kraken
quote: I tried the pork and mushroom rice, shrimp taco, pil pil prawn croquetas and duck lasagna. The food is delicious and portions are reasonable.
article_title: Where To Eat In Malaga (My Recommendations As A Local)

name: Casa Lola Strachan
quote: My go-to choice when I eat out in the historic centre. Our favourite tapas are their patatas bravas and Russian salad. We also always order the grilled monkfish that comes with salad.
article_title: Where To Eat In Malaga (My Recommendations As A Local)

name: El Pimpi
quote: You can have some of the most traditional dishes in Malaga province. The menu changes with seasons but some favourites are always available — salmorejo, ensaladilla rusa and my personal favourite, the fritura mixta.
article_title: Where To Eat In Malaga (My Recommendations As A Local)

name: FOMO Bar
quote: I love that they use local products and make traditional dishes with a modern twist without compromising flavours. The Perrito Malagueño — brioche with salchichon and Pedro Ximenez wine — is a highlight.
article_title: Where To Eat In Malaga (My Recommendations As A Local)

name: Los Mellizos
quote: This fish restaurant specialises in fried anchovies with lemon, clams, grilled white fish and paella. It also offers gluten-free dishes — just talk to the waiter to make a note.
article_title: Where To Eat In Malaga (My Recommendations As A Local)

name: Taberna La Pechá
quote: My top recommendation is to order the Ensaladilla Rusa. Personally, it's one of the best I've ever had — smooth, perfectly seasoned, a standout tapa in Málaga.
article_title: Where To Eat In Malaga (My Recommendations As A Local)

name: Los Cuñaos
quote: This restaurant serves the best fried fish and espetos in Malaga, and I love that the service is usually fast. The best chiringuito in Pedregalejo.
article_title: Where To Eat In Malaga (My Recommendations As A Local)

name: Restaurante Mexicano Tulum
quote: I usually order the cochinita pibil and chicken tacos with salsa verde. They're very flavoursome — a genuine Mexican restaurant, not a chain.
article_title: Where To Eat In Malaga (My Recommendations As A Local)

name: Parador de Málaga Gibralfaro
quote: Their take on the classic crema catalana is my favourite. It's made with mangoes from the Axarquia region in Malaga — one of the best desserts I've had in the city.
article_title: Where To Eat In Malaga (My Recommendations As A Local)

name: El Huesca
quote: High tables on the terrace, with views of the Cathedral and the Palo Borracho tree in flower; the danger of El Huesca is that hours pass and you don't notice.
article_title: Dónde comer en Málaga según la periodista y organizadora de eventos Rocío Gaspar

name: Rincón Catedral
quote: Ask for Emilia and order the flagship dish of Málaga, its majesty the Gazpachuelo. Emilia's version is unmatched — there is no better in the city.
article_title: Dónde comer en Málaga según la periodista y organizadora de eventos Rocío Gaspar

name: Restaurante El Jardín
quote: Operating since 1887, it maintains the flavour of times past. Ask about the daily menu — it is very well priced and has a varied offering.
article_title: Dónde comer en Málaga según la periodista y organizadora de eventos Rocío Gaspar

name: Araboka
quote: Owner Antonio welcomes you at the door. The ajoblanco is super famous and must-try — a cold almond-garlic soup that defines the house.
article_title: Dónde comer en Málaga según la periodista y organizadora de eventos Rocío Gaspar

name: EME de Mariano
quote: Small and charming restaurant near the Cathedral; their cachopo should be in the Top Five of Málaga's best cachopo dishes — a must for lovers of hearty traditional cooking.
article_title: Dónde comer en Málaga según la periodista y organizadora de eventos Rocío Gaspar

name: Casa Aranda
quote: Traditional churros early in the morning, when you still feel the cool breeze — the best way to start the day in Málaga according to chef Carlos Navarro.
article_title: Dónde comer en Málaga según Carlos Navarro, chef y asesor gastronómico

name: La Antxoeta
quote: Tasting the refined menu by Pablo Caballero that offers textures, flavors and presentation of home cooking elevated to its highest expression, a tribute to a lifetime exploring Mediterranean cuisine.
article_title: Dónde comer en Málaga según Carlos Navarro, chef y asesor gastronómico

name: Uvedoble
quote: Enjoy a variety of delicious tapas-style dishes such as ensaladilla rusa, smoked sardines or Iberian sirloin. The chefs use local ingredients to prepare a wide selection of mouth-watering flavours.
article_title: Which are the best restaurants in Malaga?

name: Balausta
dishes_mentioned: roasted pineapple and coconut ice cream
quote: Flavoursome dishes such as garlic prawns, warm asparagus salad and pure-bred Malaga suckling goat grace the main menu, followed by a tempting array of desserts like tiramisu or roasted pineapple and coconut ice cream.
article_title: Which are the best restaurants in Malaga?

name: Los Patios de Beatas
quote: From fried calamari to Thai-style mussels or black garlic buttered anchovies, each dish encompasses the charm of Andalusia and its delicious cuisine — accompanied by over 500 wines from the cellar.
article_title: Which are the best restaurants in Malaga?

name: La Cosmopolita
quote: Steak tartare, Malaga prawns or albondigas are just a small selection of seasonal dishes to tempt you — fresh, created with local ingredients and a touch of the exotic.
article_title: Which are the best restaurants in Malaga?

name: Ta-Kumi
quote: From sushi on rice to fresh sweet shrimp and wagyu beef, each dish is prepared with precision by Japanese chefs combining the flavours of the Far East with local Spanish ingredients.
article_title: Which are the best restaurants in Malaga?

name: El Pimpi
quote: Discover the selection of signature local dishes such as Iberian ham, bluefin tuna and rice with farm chicken on offer at this Malaga wine cellar/restaurant, located in an 18th-century building close to the Alcazaba.
article_title: Which are the best restaurants in Malaga?

name: Verum — el Asador de Málaga
quote: Serving a variety of high-quality grilled meats, from cured beef to black-legged pig from Andalusia. Accompanied by succulent beef tomatoes, traditional Padron peppers and fresh Mediterranean asparagus.
article_title: Which are the best restaurants in Malaga?

name: Beluga
quote: From Malaga rice — where Beluga gets its name — to seaweed dressed with leeks, French oysters or acorn-fed Iberian ham, the excellent cuisine takes you on a journey along the Mediterranean coastline.
article_title: Which are the best restaurants in Malaga?

name: Da Saveria
quote: Whether you adore fresh Italian pasta, pizza, chicken or fish, every dish promises a blend of exquisite flavours — eat inside or outside on the terrace opposite the Iglesia del Carmen.
article_title: Which are the best restaurants in Malaga?

name: Cávala
dishes_mentioned: regional dishes from valleys and mountains
quote: Chef Miguel Ángel Mayor showcases local commitment through dishes like gazpachuelo and urta, revealing a Málaga that extends beyond coastal areas into valleys and mountains to uncover overlooked regional treasures.
article_title: Los mejores restaurantes donde comer en Málaga

name: La Taberna de Mike Palmer
quote: Chef Miguel Palma operates this off-centre taberna emphasising Mediterranean ingredients. Featuring fuego lento y parrilla — even vegetables are grilled — alongside local specialties like chorizo croquetas and morcilla de hígado.
article_title: Los mejores restaurantes donde comer en Málaga

name: Palodú
dishes_mentioned: cocina joven y a dos voces
quote: Opened in 2015 by Cristina Cánovas and Diego Aguilar, this neighbourhood restaurant maintains an essence of local community while sourcing from surrounding shops. Known for cocina joven y a dos voces — a young kitchen, two voices.
article_title: Los mejores restaurantes donde comer en Málaga

name: Beluga
quote: Co-owned by Alicantian Diego René López and Andrea Martos since 2017, this restaurant offers a magnificent collection of arroces alongside technical preparations and an accessible wine selection with generous glass options.
article_title: Los mejores restaurantes donde comer en Málaga

name: La Cosmo
quote: Dani Carnero operates this Bib Gourmand-recognised space completing his gastronomic trio with Kaleja and La Cosmopolita. It combines classics and savoury informality with items like roasted peppers and patata rota.
article_title: Los mejores restaurantes donde comer en Málaga
